prompt:
I built a config management CLI tool and I think my README is misleading users. People keep opening issues saying 'this doesn't work' when they hit edge cases. Here's the current README intro:

"# configctl

The simplest way to manage your configuration files. Just run `configctl init` and you're done! configctl automatically detects your environment, merges your config sources, and keeps everything in sync. No setup required — it just works.

## Quick Start
`pip install configctl`
`configctl init`
That's it! Your configs are now managed.

Note: configctl supports YAML, TOML, JSON, and INI formats."

The reality is that configctl has a merge strategy system with 3 modes (overlay, deep-merge, replace), environment detection can fail on non-standard setups and needs a fallback config, and the 'init' command actually scans your whole home directory which surprises people. How do I make the docs honest without scaring people away?

assertions.text: Identifies the current README as fake simplicity — hiding real complexity (merge modes, env detection limits, init scope) behind 'it just works' language
The proposed revision mentions the 3 merge modes and helps the user understand when each applies, without making it feel intimidating
The proposed revision is honest about what 'configctl init' actually does (scans home directory) so users are not surprised
Frames the tool's configurability (multiple merge modes, format support, env detection) as a strength rather than apologizing for it
Still provides a clear, usable quick-start path — does not drown the newcomer in configuration options upfront
